name: Digital Sovereignty-Dependency Strategy
consequences: Choosing to diversify technology suppliers will likely reduce reliance on foreign platforms → Systemic: 30% decrease in supplier concentration risk → Strategic: Enhances Denmark's digital sovereignty and resilience against geopolitical shifts.
options: Engage multiple European technology providers to create a competitive ecosystem for digital identity solutions.
Establish partnerships with emerging tech firms specializing in decentralized identity solutions to reduce reliance on major platforms.
Develop a national initiative to foster local startups focused on digital identity technologies, creating a self-sustaining ecosystem.
review: Controls Supplier Concentration vs. Innovation. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential for slower implementation timelines due to the need for coalition-building.

name: Fallback Authentication-Standards Strategy
consequences: Implementing fallback authentication standards will likely enhance system resilience → Systemic: 40% improvement in service continuity during platform outages → Strategic: Establishes a robust framework for future digital identity systems.
options: Adopt existing open standards like FIDO2/WebAuthn for fallback authentication to ensure compatibility and security.
Pilot a hybrid authentication model that combines biometric and hardware-token solutions to enhance security and user experience.
Create a new open-source fallback authentication protocol that leverages blockchain technology for decentralized trust.
review: Controls Security vs. Usability. Weakness: The options may overlook user experience challenges associated with implementing new authentication methods.

name: Coalition-Building-Advocacy Strategy
consequences: Building a coalition will likely amplify advocacy efforts for platform neutrality → Systemic: 25% increase in stakeholder engagement and support → Strategic: Strengthens the political will necessary for policy changes.
options: Form a coalition with civil society organizations and privacy advocates to lobby for platform-neutral policies.
Engage academic institutions to conduct research that supports the need for a diversified digital identity infrastructure.
Launch a public awareness campaign highlighting the risks of dependency on foreign technology suppliers to mobilize citizen support.
review: Controls Influence vs. Resource Allocation. Weakness: The options may not adequately address the challenge of aligning diverse stakeholder interests.

name: Procurement-Policy Strategy
consequences: Revising procurement policies will likely ensure platform-neutral requirements are included → Systemic: 50% increase in compliance with new digital identity standards → Strategic: Positions Denmark as a leader in digital sovereignty.
options: Revise public procurement guidelines to mandate platform-neutrality in all digital identity projects.
Collaborate with EU bodies to align Danish procurement policies with broader European digital identity frameworks.
Introduce incentives for public-sector projects that prioritize open-source and platform-neutral solutions.
review: Controls Compliance vs. Innovation. Weakness: The options may face resistance from established suppliers accustomed to existing procurement practices.

name: Technical Demonstration-Validation Strategy
consequences: Developing technical demonstrators will likely showcase feasibility of alternatives → Systemic: 60% increase in stakeholder confidence in platform-neutral solutions → Strategic: Provides tangible evidence to influence policy and procurement decisions.
options: Create a mobile-oriented demonstrator that utilizes open standards to authenticate against a simulated public-sector environment.
Develop a browser-based fallback authentication demonstrator that operates independently of mobile OS platforms.
Leverage emerging technologies like decentralized identifiers (DIDs) to create a next-generation authentication demonstrator.
review: Controls Innovation vs. Practicality. Weakness: The options may not fully account for the complexities of integrating with existing systems.

name: Resilience-Framework Strategy
consequences: Adopting a resilience framework will likely enhance system robustness against disruptions → Systemic: 35% reduction in service downtime during crises → Strategic: Establishes a proactive approach to digital identity management.
options: Implement a risk assessment framework to identify vulnerabilities in current digital identity systems and propose mitigations.
Create a resilience training program for stakeholders to prepare for potential disruptions in digital identity services.
Develop a comprehensive continuity plan that outlines fallback procedures for critical public services during platform failures.
review: Controls Risk Management vs. Resource Allocation. Weakness: The options may not sufficiently address the urgency of immediate implementation.

name: Interoperability-Integration Strategy
consequences: Focusing on interoperability will likely facilitate smoother transitions between systems → Systemic: 45% increase in cross-platform compatibility → Strategic: Enhances user trust and adoption of digital identity solutions.
options: Establish technical standards for interoperability between existing and new digital identity systems to ensure seamless integration.
Collaborate with international bodies to align Danish digital identity standards with global interoperability frameworks.
Pilot integration projects that connect Danish digital identity solutions with other European systems to demonstrate feasibility.
review: Controls Standardization vs. Flexibility. Weakness: The options may overlook the complexities of aligning diverse international standards.

name: Public Engagement-Transparency Strategy
consequences: Enhancing public engagement will likely increase citizen trust in digital identity initiatives → Systemic: 30% improvement in public perception of digital identity security → Strategic: Fosters a collaborative environment for policy development.
options: Launch a series of public forums to educate citizens about the importance of digital identity and gather feedback on proposed changes.
Create an online platform for citizens to voice concerns and suggestions regarding digital identity policies and practices.
Develop informational materials that clearly explain the benefits and risks associated with platform-neutral digital identity solutions.
review: Controls Transparency vs. Control. Weakness: The options may not adequately address the challenge of misinformation and public skepticism.

name: Innovation-Research Strategy
consequences: Investing in research will likely drive technological advancements in digital identity solutions → Systemic: 50% increase in innovative proposals for platform-neutral technologies → Strategic: Positions Denmark as a leader in digital identity innovation.
options: Fund research grants for academic institutions to explore new technologies in digital identity and authentication methods.
Establish partnerships with tech incubators to support startups focused on developing platform-neutral identity solutions.
Create a national research consortium to collaborate on innovative digital identity projects and share findings.
review: Controls Investment vs. Immediate Needs. Weakness: The options may not prioritize short-term deliverables necessary for project success.

name: Regulatory-Framework Strategy
consequences: Revising the regulatory framework will likely streamline compliance for digital identity initiatives → Systemic: 40% reduction in regulatory bottlenecks for new projects → Strategic: Enhances agility in adapting to technological changes and market needs.
options: Conduct a comprehensive review of existing regulations affecting digital identity to identify and eliminate unnecessary barriers.
Propose new regulatory guidelines that encourage innovation while ensuring security and privacy in digital identity solutions.
Engage with legal experts to draft flexible regulatory frameworks that can adapt to emerging technologies in digital identity.
review: Controls Compliance vs. Innovation. Weakness: The options may not fully consider the potential pushback from established regulatory bodies.

name: Stakeholder-Collaboration Strategy
consequences: Fostering stakeholder collaboration will likely enhance collective input on digital identity solutions → Systemic: 30% increase in stakeholder satisfaction and engagement → Strategic: Builds a unified front for advocating platform-neutral policies.
options: Organize regular roundtable discussions with key stakeholders to align interests and share insights on digital identity challenges.
Create a collaborative online workspace for stakeholders to contribute ideas and resources related to digital identity initiatives.
Develop joint initiatives with stakeholders to pilot new digital identity solutions and gather real-world feedback.
review: Controls Inclusivity vs. Decision-Making Speed. Weakness: The options may slow down decision-making processes due to the need for consensus.

name: Technology-Assessment Strategy
consequences: Conducting technology assessments will likely identify viable alternatives to current platforms → Systemic: 25% increase in the identification of suitable technologies for digital identity → Strategic: Informs strategic decisions on technology adoption and integration.
options: Perform a comprehensive assessment of emerging technologies that could serve as alternatives to Apple and Google for digital identity solutions.
Engage independent experts to evaluate the security and usability of proposed digital identity technologies.
Create a technology evaluation framework that includes criteria for platform neutrality and resilience.
review: Controls Innovation vs. Risk Aversion. Weakness: The options may not adequately address the urgency of immediate technological needs.

name: Funding-Resource Strategy
consequences: Securing diverse funding sources will likely enhance project sustainability and scope → Systemic: 40% increase in available resources for digital identity initiatives → Strategic: Reduces reliance on single funding sources and enhances project resilience.
options: Explore public-private partnerships to secure additional funding for digital identity projects and initiatives.
Apply for EU grants focused on digital identity and cybersecurity to supplement national funding efforts.
Develop a crowdfunding campaign to engage citizens and raise awareness while generating financial support for the project.
review: Controls Financial Stability vs. Project Scope. Weakness: The options may not sufficiently address the complexities of managing multiple funding sources.

name: User-Centric-Design Strategy
consequences: Implementing user-centric design will likely improve the usability of digital identity solutions → Systemic: 35% increase in user satisfaction and adoption rates → Strategic: Ensures that solutions meet the needs of diverse user groups.
options: Conduct user research to gather insights on citizen needs and preferences regarding digital identity solutions.
Develop prototypes based on user feedback to iteratively refine digital identity interfaces and functionalities.
Establish a user advisory board to provide ongoing input and guidance on digital identity initiatives.
review: Controls Usability vs. Technical Feasibility. Weakness: The options may not fully account for the technical limitations of existing systems.

name: Crisis-Management Strategy
consequences: Developing a crisis management strategy will likely enhance preparedness for potential disruptions → Systemic: 50% improvement in response times during digital identity crises → Strategic: Establishes a proactive approach to managing risks associated with digital identity systems.
options: Create a crisis response team dedicated to addressing potential failures in digital identity systems and ensuring continuity of service.
Develop a communication plan for informing stakeholders and the public during crises related to digital identity services.
Conduct regular crisis simulation exercises to test the effectiveness of response strategies and improve readiness.
review: Controls Preparedness vs. Resource Allocation. Weakness: The options may not sufficiently address the need for immediate action in the face of emerging threats.
